old_claim.note: Graphical User Interface (GUI) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the 1968 head-mounted display depended on electronics, software, tracking, and computer graphics rather than a general GUI software paradigm.

source_shape.source_locator: Sutherland describes a head-mounted three-dimensional computer display with perspective changing according to the viewer's position, not a desktop graphical user interface.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports computer-generated 3D display and tracking as the early VR anchor while not requiring GUI software.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping GUI direct would import later desktop interaction conventions into an earlier immersive display lineage.

ontology_before: Virtual reality depended directly on graphical_user_interface_gui.
ontology_after: Virtual reality is scoped to immersive computer-simulated environments anchored by the Sutherland head-mounted display and no longer requires a GUI prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: graphical_user_interface_gui is no longer a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to desktop-windowed virtual-world software rather than immersive VR systems.
A source shows Sutherland-style or early immersive VR required GUI software as a direct enabling technology.
A dependent needs virtual_reality to imply GUI application software rather than immersive computer-generated environments.
short_reason: Early computer VR needed computer graphics and display/tracking hardware, not a GUI prerequisite.
